title: Queue Settings
lede: Never lose a caller to a busy team again. Queues hold your customers with music, live place-in-line updates, and a guaranteed safety net — this guide walks you through every queue setting, from max wait to the AI fallback, plus what your agents see on their side.

steps.title: Understand how queues work in iMash
steps.content:
A queue is a polite holding room: the caller hears music and spoken updates while iMash keeps ringing your team's Online members — the moment an agent frees up, the call connects. A caller lands in a queue in two ways:

  • Someone parks them there on purpose. During a live call, an agent (or an AI assistant) transfers the customer to a group as a queue — for example, handing a billing question to the Billing team's queue.
  • Inbound overflow. A call rings the group but every member is busy — with the overflow toggle on (it is by default), the caller is held in the queue instead of being bounced straight to the fallback.

Callers are answered in the order they arrived, and the queue can even tell each caller their live place in line. Everything on this page is configured per group, so Sales and Support can each have their own wait limits, sounds, and fallbacks.

steps.title: Open the Queue Settings tab for a group
steps.content:
1. In the left sidebar, under Call Center, click Contact Center.
  2. Open the Groups tab at the top of the page.
  3. In the Groups list on the left, click the group you want to configure.
  4. In the group panel, open the Queue Settings tab (you may need to scroll the tab strip to the right).

You'll see one card — Queue (transfer to this group as a queue) — with the wait limit, the timeout action, and the on-hold audio for this group's queue.

images.alt: Contact Center Groups tab with a group selected and the Queue Settings tab open
images.caption: Groups → select a group → Queue Settings: max wait, timeout action, and queue audio in one card.

images.alt: Contact Center Groups tab with a group selected and the Queue Settings tab open
images.caption: Groups → select a group → Queue Settings: max wait, timeout action, and queue audio in one card.

steps.title: Set the maximum wait time
steps.content:
In Max wait in queue (seconds), enter how long a queued customer can wait before the timeout action (next step) fires.

  • 300 (5 minutes) is a solid starting point for most teams.
  • Enter 0 to use the system default — 30 minutes.
  • The maximum you can set is 14400 seconds (4 hours).
💡 Think of this as your customer-patience budget. Long enough that a briefly busy team can still catch the call, short enough that nobody stews on hold — 300–600 seconds works well for most support and sales teams.

images.alt: Queue Settings card showing Max wait in queue, timeout action, and audio toggles
images.caption: The queue card: Max wait in queue (seconds), the timeout action, and the audio toggles.

images.alt: Queue Settings card showing Max wait in queue, timeout action, and audio toggles
images.caption: The queue card: Max wait in queue (seconds), the timeout action, and the audio toggles.

steps.title: Choose what happens when the max wait is reached
steps.content:
Under When the max wait is reached, pick the safety net for a caller nobody managed to answer in time:

  • Hang up the call — the call simply ends after the max wait.
  • Transfer to an AI assistant — the customer is handed to an AI agent instead of being dropped. A new field appears: AI assistant to take the call — pick the assistant (this field is required when this option is selected).
💡 The AI handoff is the difference between "we missed a call" and "we captured a lead": the assistant can apologize for the wait, answer questions, take a detailed message, or book a callback — 24/7, even when the whole team is slammed.

images.alt: When the max wait is reached set to Transfer to an AI assistant with the assistant picker visible
images.caption: Choose Transfer to an AI assistant and the required assistant picker appears.

images.alt: When the max wait is reached set to Transfer to an AI assistant with the assistant picker visible
images.caption: Choose Transfer to an AI assistant and the required assistant picker appears.

steps.title: Shape what callers hear while they wait
steps.content:
Two controls decide the on-hold soundtrack for this queue:

  • Play hold music while queued — plays the Hold music (or Wait music) you configured on the Wait & Hold experience tab. Turn it off for silence between the spoken messages.
  • Queue periodic messages — spoken lines that loop while the caller is held in this queue, separate from the wait messages. Leave the list empty to reuse the wait messages instead.

With Queue periodic messages switched on:

  1. Tick Play the first message once before the hold music starts if you want message 1 to act as a one-time greeting (it's tagged Before music).
  2. Type each message — for example, "Thanks for waiting, {first_name}."
  3. Next to each message, set the wait before it plays: a single number of seconds (e.g. 30) or a range like 15-45 for a random wait each time.
  4. Click Add message for more lines; the trash icon removes one.
💡 Personalization tokens work here: {first_name} greets the caller by name, and {callers_queue_position} updates live with their place in line — "You're number 2 in line" beats generic hold music every single time.

images.alt: Queue periodic messages enabled with the message list, interval field, and Add message button
images.caption: Queue periodic messages: intro-before-music checkbox, message rows with per-message wait, and Add message.

images.alt: Queue periodic messages enabled with the message list, interval field, and Add message button
images.caption: Queue periodic messages: intro-before-music checkbox, message rows with per-message wait, and Add message.

steps.title: Decide if busy inbound calls queue automatically
steps.content:
There's one more queue switch, and it lives on a different tab: open the group's Ring strategy tab and find the Call handling card.

  • Hold callers in a queue when no agent is free — On (default): if every member is busy, the inbound caller is held in the queue (hold music + place in line) and rings the moment an agent frees up, up to the queue max-wait — then the When no one answers fallback runs. Off: the call rings the online members once; if nobody answers, the fallback runs immediately — callers are never queued.
⚠️ Turning this off means a busy moment sends callers straight to the fallback (AI agent or hang-up). Keep it on unless you specifically want a "ring once, then AI" flow.

images.alt: Call handling card on the Ring strategy tab with the Hold callers in a queue toggle enabled
images.caption: Ring strategy → Call handling: 'Hold callers in a queue when no agent is free' controls inbound overflow queuing.

images.alt: Call handling card on the Ring strategy tab with the Hold callers in a queue toggle enabled
images.caption: Ring strategy → Call handling: 'Hold callers in a queue when no agent is free' controls inbound overflow queuing.

steps.title: Save and test your queue
steps.content:
1. Click Save at the bottom right of the group panel — one save commits every tab's settings for the group.
  2. To test: make sure at least one member is Online, then have an agent transfer a live call to this group as a queue (or call one of the group's inbound numbers while everyone is busy).
  3. Watch the Live Monitor tab — queued customers appear in the On Queue panel in real time, so you can see the queue working the moment you set it up.
💡 How members are rung out of the queue follows the group's Ring strategy — ring all at once, round-robin, linear, or longest idle. Tune it on the Ring strategy tab.

steps.title: Know what your agents see
steps.content:
Here's the queue from your team's side of the glass:

  • The On Queue panel in the middle of the agent home screen shows every customer currently waiting, with a live count.
  • Agents don't "pick" from the queue — while they're Online, the system automatically rings the next queued customer to them. When nobody is waiting, the panel shows No customers on queue.
  • To park a customer in a queue, an agent on a live call clicks Teammate in the web dialer, which opens Conference / Transfer, and picks A queue — then chooses the group. The customer hears that group's hold music and messages while its online members are rung.

That's it — your queue now catches every caller, entertains them by name, and always has an AI backstop. For the full day-in-the-life walkthrough your reps should read, see the Agent Guide below.

images.alt: Agent home screen with the On Queue panel, web dialer, and Online status
images.caption: The agent view: On Queue panel in the middle, web dialer with the Teammate button on the right, and the Online status card.

images.alt: Agent home screen with the On Queue panel, web dialer, and Online status
images.caption: The agent view: On Queue panel in the middle, web dialer with the Teammate button on the right, and the Online status card.

support.title: Need Help?
support.content:
If you run into any issues or have questions:

  • 📧 Email us at support@imash.io
  • 💬 Join our Discord community
  • 📚 Check out our API Reference

metaDescription: Configure Contact Center queue settings in iMash: max wait time, hang up or AI-assistant timeout action, hold music, live place-in-line periodic messages, inbound overflow queuing, and the agent-side On Queue experience.
